summary: Mellow Dylan set with standout Rainy Day Women and Frankie Lee
blurb: The third set’s Dylan‑Dead collaboration delivers the show’s high points, highlighted by a rousing "Rainy Day Women #12 & 35" and a moving "Ballad Of Frankie Lee And Judas Priest." Earlier sets are more subdued, with solid playing on "Iko Iko" and "Let It Grow," but the rhythm section feels low‑energy at times.
review: July 19, 1987 at Autzen Stadium is remembered primarily for its historic Dylan/Dead third set. Reviewers rave about the magical chemistry on songs like "The Ballad Of Frankie Lee And Judas Priest," "Rainy Day Women #12 & 35," and "Queen Jane Approximately," calling the performance near‑perfect and a highlight of the band’s late‑80s Dylan collaborations. The first two sets are more relaxed: “Iko Iko” opens with fresh vocals from Jerry, while “Let It Grow” and “Heart Of Mine” provide pleasant moments, though the overall pacing feels mellow and the rhythm section occasionally drifts, leaving some listeners wishing for a stronger bass‑drum drive. Brent’s keyboards add extra sparkle on "Lovelight" (the set’s “Turn On Your Lovelight”), but the show’s greatest excitement comes from the seamless blend of folk‑rock material with classic Dead improvisation in the final half hour.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Six of seven recordings are positive, praising the third set’s Dylan/Dead collaboration and Jerry’s guitar tone; only one analysis is mixed, noting bass‑shy soundboard issues. The consistent acclaim for multiple songs across recordings justifies a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Good soundboard source with clear capture of the entire show; despite slight bass‑shyness it presents the full setlist and highlights without dropouts.

key_highlights: Third‑set Dylan/Dead collaboration shines, especially "Rainy Day Women #12 & 35" and "Ballad Of Frankie Lee And Judas Priest".
Jerry’s fresh vocal on "Iko Iko" and mesmerizing guitar tone throughout the show.
Brent adds energetic keyboard touches on "Turn On Your Lovelight".
Overall recording is good, though bass‑shy moments make the rhythm section feel subdued.
song_highlights: The Ballad Of Frankie Lee And Judas Priest
Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again

band_performance.Jerry: Fresh vocal on "Iko Iko" and consistently praised for his mesmerizing guitar tone; solos are described as safe yet competent, with many noting his irreplaceable presence in the third set.
band_performance.Phil: Solid bass foundation, though a few reviewers wish for more presence in the mix during the second set.
band_performance.Brent: Keyboard work adds energy on "Turn On Your Lovelight" and supports the Dylan/Dead material with bright comping, especially noticeable on "Lovelight" moments.
band_performance.Mickey: Drumming feels low‑energy; one reviewer wonders where the drummers are during much of the show.
band_performance.Billy: Same as Mickey – rhythm section appears subdued, contributing to an overall mellow feel.

must_listen_sequences: The Ballad Of Frankie Lee And Judas Priest
Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again
